items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: the role of oral traditions in preserving cultural knowledge
items.transcript: Before writing existed, and in many communities long after it arrived, human knowledge survived through a single fragile channel: the human voice. Anthropologists study this as oral tradition, the practice of passing down history, law, genealogy, and technical know-how by spoken word from one generation to the next. It's easy to assume that a society without writing must have a shaky grip on its own past. But oral cultures developed remarkably reliable techniques for keeping their knowledge intact. Consider the griots of West Africa, professional oral historians who could recite the genealogies of ruling families going back centuries. A griot didn't simply remember facts. He wrapped them in rhythm, melody, and fixed poetic formulas, and those structures acted like scaffolding. Rhyme and meter, it turns out, are not mere decoration; they are error-correcting devices. When a line must scan a certain way, a mistaken word breaks the pattern, and the performer or the audience notices. Anthropologists have also found that oral knowledge is often distributed rather than centralized. Different specialists guard different bodies of lore, so no single failure erases everything. What emerges from all this is a reappraisal. For a long time scholars treated oral societies as primitive stages on the road to literacy. We now see something more interesting: sophisticated systems engineered, in their own way, for accuracy and endurance. Writing changed what humans could store, but it did not invent the human capacity to preserve knowledge across time. That capacity was already there, carried in performance, memory, and the disciplined art of the spoken word.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How oral traditions can reliably preserve knowledge without writing
options.B: The genealogies of West African ruling families and their griots
options.C: How the invention of writing transformed early human societies
options.D: Why traditional poetry relies on rhyme and meter for beauty

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是无文字社会如何通过口述传统可靠地保存知识，A准确概括。B抓住griot和统治家族谱系这一具体例子，属于too_narrow。C谈文字的发明如何改变社会，讲座恰恰强调文字并非保存知识的唯一途径，属于wrong_topic。D借用讲座中rhyme和meter的词汇，但把它们说成为了艺术美感，方向错误，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do rhyme and meter help preserve oral knowledge?

options.A: They make the recitations more entertaining for large audiences
options.B: They break the pattern when a word is wrong, revealing errors
options.C: They let a single griot store all of a society's lore
options.D: They were added purely as decoration by later performers

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说韵律和格律是纠错装置，一旦用词出错就会破坏节奏模式而被察觉，B正确。A说它们只是让表演更有趣，讲座没这么讲，属于topic_adjacent。C说单个griot能存储整个社会的知识，与讲座强调知识分散保管相矛盾，属于reversed_logic。D说韵律只是后来表演者添加的装饰，与讲座所说它们绝非装饰而是纠错装置正好相反，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor point out that oral knowledge is often distributed among specialists?

options.A: To argue that griots were unable to memorize anything alone
options.B: To state that different specialists guarded different bodies of lore
options.C: To explain why no single failure could erase all the knowledge
options.D: To show that oral societies lacked any central authority

questions.explanation: 教授指出口述知识分散保管，是为了说明不会因某一个人的失误而全部丢失，C正确。A说griot独自完全记不住任何东西，是教授未表达的夸大引申，属于over_inference。B只是复述不同专家守护不同知识这一内容，没解释用意，属于literal_restatement。D把它读成口述社会缺乏中央权威，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the traditional scholarly view of oral societies?

options.A: It correctly ranked oral cultures as an early stage of literacy
options.B: It had proven that every oral society eventually adopted writing
options.C: It focused mostly on the artistic quality of oral performances
options.D: It underestimated how sophisticated these knowledge systems were

questions.explanation: 由讲座可推断，传统学界把口述社会看作走向文字的原始阶段，低估了这些知识系统的精巧，D正确。A说这一旧观点是正确的，与教授明确的重新评价相反，属于reversed_logic。B说它证明每个口述社会最终都采用了文字，过度绝对化，属于over_generalization。C说旧观点主要关注表演的艺术性，是相关但讲座未支持的推断，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: Think back to a vacation you took, or a long visit to the dentist. When you recall it later, you don't replay every single moment. Instead, your memory of the whole experience is dominated by just two points. Psychologists call this the peak-end rule. The idea, developed by Daniel Kahneman and his colleagues, is that we judge an experience largely by how it felt at its most intense moment, the peak, and by how it felt at the very end, rather than by averaging every moment along the way. In one striking study, patients undergoing an uncomfortable medical procedure were split into two groups. For one group, the procedure was left unchanged. For the other, doctors added a few extra minutes at the end during which the discomfort was mild rather than sharp. Objectively, that second group endured more total discomfort, since their procedure lasted longer. Yet afterward they remembered the whole experience as less unpleasant, and they were more willing to return. Why? Because the ending was gentler, and the ending carries disproportionate weight. What's remarkable here is the gap this reveals between two different selves: the self that lives through an experience moment by moment, and the self that remembers it afterward. These two don't always agree. The remembering self, which drives our future decisions, cares intensely about peaks and endings and is surprisingly indifferent to how long something lasted. That's why a brief but terrible finish can spoil an otherwise pleasant day, and why a rough experience that ends well can be recalled almost fondly.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How doctors can make medical procedures shorter and less painful
options.B: A rule describing how peaks and endings shape our memories
options.C: The results of a single study on patients' medical discomfort
options.D: Why long vacations often feel much shorter than they really were

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是peak-end rule，即人对经历的记忆主要由最强烈的时刻和结尾决定，B正确。A谈如何让医疗过程更短更轻松，讲座只是借医疗研究举例，主题并非改进治疗，属于wrong_topic。C只抓住那个医疗研究，属于too_narrow。D借用讲座开头的vacation一词，但方向完全错误，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the study find about the group whose procedure was extended?

options.A: They felt sharper, more intense pain throughout the whole procedure
options.B: They later asked their doctors to shorten all future procedures
options.C: They remembered the experience as less unpleasant overall
options.D: They endured less total discomfort than the unchanged group

questions.explanation: 讲座说被延长治疗的那组虽然总体不适更多，但事后却把整个经历记得更轻松，C正确。A说他们全程感到更剧烈的疼痛，与讲座所述结尾更温和相矛盾，属于wrong_detail。B说他们要求缩短未来治疗，讲座没提，属于topic_adjacent。D说他们承受的总不适更少，与讲座明说他们持续时间更长、总不适更多正好相反，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor distinguish between the experiencing self and the remembering self?

options.A: To recommend that people simply trust their in-the-moment feelings
options.B: To prove that human memory is completely unreliable in general
options.C: To restate that each person happens to have two different selves
options.D: To explain why our memories can conflict with what we actually felt

questions.explanation: 教授区分体验自我与记忆自我，是为了解释为何我们的记忆会与当时的真实感受不一致，D正确。A说建议人们只相信当下感受，误判目的，属于wrong_purpose。B说这证明人的记忆完全不可靠，过度引申，属于over_inference。C只是复述人有两个自我这一说法，没解释用意，属于literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how the duration of an experience affects our memory of it?

options.A: It matters far less than the peak and the ending do
options.B: It has absolutely no influence on any decision we ever make
options.C: It shapes our memory even more strongly than the ending does
options.D: It determines how much physical pain a patient is able to tolerate

questions.explanation: 由讲座可推断，经历持续多久对记忆的影响远小于峰值和结尾，A正确。B说时长对任何决定都毫无影响，过度绝对化，属于over_generalization。C说时长比结尾更强地塑造记忆，与讲座强调结尾权重过大正好相反，属于reversed_logic。D说时长决定患者能忍受多少疼痛，是相关但讲座未支持的推断，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: the role of wetlands in flood prevention
items.transcript: When a river swells after heavy rain, we often picture concrete dams and levees holding the water back. But some of the most effective flood defenses aren't built by engineers at all. They're wetlands, the marshes, swamps, and bogs that sit along rivers and coastlines. A wetland is land where the soil is saturated with water either permanently or seasonally, and in flood terms it behaves like a giant sponge. Here's how it works. During a heavy storm, a wetland soaks up enormous volumes of water into its spongy soils and dense vegetation. Instead of rushing downstream all at once, the water is held and then released slowly over days or weeks. This flattens the flood peak, the dangerous surge that would otherwise overwhelm towns downstream. Consider coastal marshes during storms. Their thick grasses and muddy ground absorb the force of incoming surges, so less water and energy reach the land behind them. Studies after major hurricanes have estimated that coastal wetlands prevent billions of dollars in flood damage every year. And unlike a concrete barrier, a wetland maintains itself and can even improve with time. The lesson for planners is significant. For decades, wetlands were drained and filled to make room for farms and housing, and only later did communities discover that they had removed a natural buffer. Today many regions are restoring wetlands precisely for their flood protection, alongside the wildlife habitat and water filtering they also provide. Sometimes the smartest engineering is simply letting a natural system do what it has always done.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How engineers design concrete dams and levees for rivers
options.B: The damage that major hurricanes cause to coastal towns
options.C: How wetlands protect nearby areas from flooding naturally
options.D: How wetlands are drained to create new farmland and housing

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是湿地如何作为天然屏障防洪，C正确。A谈工程师如何设计大坝和堤防，讲座恰恰对比这些人造设施，属于wrong_topic。B抓住飓风破坏这一细节，且方向偏离，属于too_narrow。D借用讲座后段排干湿地的说法，但那只是引出教训的背景，不是主旨，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does a wetland do with storm water?

options.A: It channels the water downstream as quickly as it can
options.B: It filters out pollutants before letting the water go
options.C: It evaporates most of the water back into the air
options.D: It soaks the water up and then releases it slowly over time

questions.explanation: 讲座明说湿地像海绵一样吸收暴雨的水，再在数天或数周内缓慢释放，D正确。A说湿地尽快把水排向下游，与讲座所述缓慢释放正好相反，属于reversed_logic。B说湿地会先过滤污染物再放水，虽是湿地相关功能但讲座此处未讲，属于topic_adjacent。C说湿地把大部分水蒸发回空气，改变了讲座的关键事实，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare a wetland to a giant sponge?

options.A: To help explain how wetlands absorb and slowly release water
options.B: To describe the muddy texture of the soil found in marshes
options.C: To argue that wetlands are softer than any concrete barrier
options.D: To suggest that a wetland can absorb an unlimited amount of water

questions.explanation: 教授把湿地比作巨型海绵，是为了形象说明它如何吸水再缓慢释放，A正确。B只是复述沼泽土壤的质地，没解释比喻的用意，属于literal_restatement。C说湿地比混凝土更软，误判比喻目的，属于wrong_purpose。D说湿地能吸收无限量的水，是教授未表达的过度引申，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what have studies after major hurricanes estimated?

options.A: That coastal wetlands are destroyed and washed away by every large storm
options.B: That coastal wetlands prevent billions of dollars in flood damage
options.C: That most coastal towns have already restored their lost marshes
options.D: That concrete levees clearly outperform wetlands during major storms

questions.explanation: 讲座说飓风后的研究估计沿海湿地每年可避免数十亿美元的洪灾损失，B正确。A说湿地会被每场大风暴摧毁冲走，与讲座强调湿地能自我维持相反，属于reversed_logic。C说多数沿海城镇已恢复湿地，讲座只说许多地区正在恢复，属于topic_adjacent。D说混凝土堤防明显优于湿地，与讲座主旨相悖，属于wrong_detail。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
items.transcript: Stand in front of a medieval painting and something may strike you as slightly off. Figures seem to float, distant objects are the same size as near ones, and the space feels flat, almost like a stage backdrop. Then, in early fifteenth-century Italy, something changed. Painters rediscovered a technique called linear perspective, a mathematical system for representing three-dimensional space on a flat surface. The core idea is that parallel lines appear to converge as they recede, meeting at a single vanishing point on the horizon. The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is usually credited with working out the geometry around 1420, and his friend Leon Battista Alberti soon wrote it down as a set of rules any painter could follow. Suddenly a painted room could seem to open up behind the picture's surface, inviting the viewer's eye to travel into a convincing depth. I say rediscovered deliberately. The ancient Greeks and Romans had grasped aspects of perspective, but that knowledge faded during the medieval centuries. What the Renaissance added was rigor: perspective became a precise, teachable geometry rather than an intuitive trick. And notice what this reflects. Perspective wasn't just a painting technique; it's an expression of a new confidence that the visible world could be measured and mastered through reason, the very spirit driving Renaissance science and mathematics. A painting organized around a vanishing point places the individual viewer at a single, privileged point of view. In that sense, the humble vanishing point mirrors a much larger shift in how Europeans were beginning to see themselves and their place in the world.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the ancient Greeks and Romans painted realistic scenes
options.B: Why figures in medieval paintings appear to float in space
options.C: How Brunelleschi designed buildings in fifteenth-century Italy
options.D: The rediscovery of linear perspective and what it reflected

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是文艺复兴早期对线性透视的重新发现及其反映的时代精神，D正确。A谈古希腊罗马如何画写实场景，只是讲座顺带提及，非主题，属于wrong_topic。B抓住中世纪人物漂浮这一细节，属于too_narrow。C借用Brunelleschi这个名字，但他设计建筑并非讲座重点，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say perspective was 'rediscovered' rather than invented?

options.A: Because earlier civilizations had used it before the knowledge was lost
options.B: Because Renaissance artists secretly disliked all newly invented things
options.C: To praise Brunelleschi for inventing a completely new geometry
options.D: To describe how parallel lines meet at a single vanishing point

questions.explanation: 教授用重新发现而非发明，是因为古代文明曾掌握透视，只是那知识在中世纪失传，A正确。B说文艺复兴艺术家私下厌恶一切新发明，是讲座未表达的臆断，属于over_inference。C说是为了赞扬Brunelleschi发明全新几何，既误判目的又与重新发现矛盾，属于wrong_purpose。D只是复述平行线汇聚于消失点这一机制，与为何用重新发现无关，属于literal_restatement。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Leon Battista Alberti contribute?

options.A: He first worked out the geometry of perspective around 1420
options.B: He wrote perspective down as rules any painter could follow
options.C: He painted the first room with a convincing vanishing point
options.D: He argued that perspective should be kept secret from painters

questions.explanation: 讲座说Alberti把透视写成一套任何画家都能遵循的规则，B正确。A说他在1420年前后首先算出透视几何，那其实是Brunelleschi的功劳，属于wrong_detail。C说他画出第一个有说服力消失点的房间，讲座没提，属于topic_adjacent。D说他主张对画家保密透视法，与他公开写成规则正好相反，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the significance of the vanishing point?

options.A: It proves that Renaissance painting was superior to all art that came later
options.B: It shows that Renaissance artists rejected reason and measurement
options.C: It reflects a new confidence that the world could be measured
options.D: It mainly influenced the later development of modern architecture

questions.explanation: 由讲座可推断，消失点反映出一种相信世界可被理性测量和掌握的新信念，C正确。A说它证明文艺复兴绘画优于此后一切艺术，过度夸大，属于over_generalization。B说文艺复兴艺术家排斥理性与测量，与讲座所述正好相反，属于reversed_logic。D说它主要影响了现代建筑的发展，是相关但讲座未支持的推断，属于topic_adjacent。
